title: Status and Error (17 commands)
body: Use the commands in the Status and Error command Group to determine the status of the instrument and control events. Several commands and queries used with the instrument are common to all devices. The IEEE Std 488.2-1987 defines these commands and queries. The common commands begin with an asterisk (*) character.

title: ⚠️ CRITICAL NOTES
body:
- **ONLY for MSO/DPO 5k/7k/70k** (Legacy scopes)
- **DOES NOT WORK on MSO5/6/MSO6B** (Modern scopes)
- Requires FILESYSTEM:READFILE command
- C:/TekScope/Temp path is proven to work on legacy scopes
- Use `time.sleep(1.0)` instead of `*OPC?` to avoid Unicode decode errors

---

title: Error: UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xe5...
body:
**Cause:** Using `query('*OPC?')` after FILESYSTEM:READFILE - the PNG data is still in the buffer

**Solution:** Use `time.sleep(1.0)` instead of `*OPC?` after HARDCOPY START or SAVE:IMAGE

title: Error: OSError: exception: access violation reading 0x...
body:
**Cause:** FILESYSTEM:READFILE called too soon before save completed

**Solution:** Add `time.sleep(1.0)` after `SAVE:IMAGE` or `HARDCOPY START` command

title: Error: Timeout on HARDCOPY:DATA?
body:
**Cause:** Using HARDCOPY:DATA? on modern scope (MSO5/6)

**Solution:** Use SAVE:IMAGE + FILESYSTEM instead (modern scopes don't support HARDCOPY)

title: Error: HARDCOPY:FORMAT ${format}
body:
**Cause:** Bug in code generator (now fixed)

**Solution:** Update pythonGenerators.ts line 1264

---

title: Recommended Improvements:
body:
1. ✅ Fix HARDCOPY:FORMAT bug (DONE)
2. ⚠️ Consider adding HARDCOPY:DATA? for legacy scopes (faster)
3. ✅ Document scope type compatibility clearly

---

title: State Flow
body:
```
TEKEXP:STATE RUN
       ↓
TEKEXP:STATE? → RUNNING | WAIT | ERROR | COMPLETE
       ↓
If WAIT/ERROR:
    TEKEXP:POPUP? → Get popup message
    TEKEXP:POPUP "OK" → Respond to popup
       ↓
Loop back to STATE? until COMPLETE
```

title: 4. Wait/Poll State Block
body:
**Block Type:** `tekexp_wait_state`

**Fields:**
- QUERY: `TEKEXP:STATE?`
- EXPECTED_VALUES: `COMPLETE`, `DONE`
- POLL_INTERVAL: Default 2 seconds
- TIMEOUT: Default 300 seconds

**Generated Python:**
```python
import time
start_time = time.time()
while True:
    state = tekexp.query("TEKEXP:STATE?").strip()
    if state in ("COMPLETE", "DONE"):
        break
    if state in ("WAIT", "ERROR"):
        # Handle popup
        popup = tekexp.query("TEKEXP:POPUP?")
        print(f"Popup: {popup}")
        tekexp.write('TEKEXP:POPUP "OK"')
    if time.time() - start_time > timeout:
        raise TimeoutError("TekExpress did not complete in time")
    time.sleep(poll_interval)
```

title: Steps UI JSON Structure
Error Handling
body:
```python
try:
    tekexp = rm.open_resource(f"TCPIP::{host}::5000::SOCKET")
    tekexp.write_termination = "\n"
    tekexp.read_termination = "\n"
    tekexp.timeout = 30000
    
    # Run test
    tekexp.write("TEKEXP:STATE RUN")
    
    # Wait with timeout
    start_time = time.time()
    while True:
        state = tekexp.query("TEKEXP:STATE?").strip()
        if state == "COMPLETE":
            print("Test completed successfully")
            break
        if state == "ERROR":
            error = tekexp.query("TEKEXP:LASTERROR?")
            raise RuntimeError(f"TekExpress error: {error}")
        if time.time() - start_time > 300:
            raise TimeoutError("Test timed out after 5 minutes")
        time.sleep(2)
        
except pyvisa.VisaIOError as e:
    print(f"Connection error: {e}")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error: {e}")
finally:
    tekexp.close()
```

---

title: SCPI Notation
body:
<NR1> = integer         e.g. 50
<NR2> = decimal         e.g. 1.0, 1e-6
<NR3> = scientific      e.g. 1.5E-6
<QString> = quoted      e.g. "C:/path/file.png"
{A|B} = choose one
[x] = optional
CH<x>       = CH1,CH2,CH3,CH4
B<x>        = B1..B16
MEAS<x>     = MEAS1,MEAS2...
MATH<x>     = MATH1,MATH2...
REF<x>      = REF1..REF4
SEARCH<x>   = SEARCH1,SEARCH2...
WAVEView<x> = WAVEView1 only
PLOTView<x> = PLOTView1 only
ZOOM<x>     = ZOOM1 only
NaN         = 9.91E+37 (query error)

title: 5) Golden Chunk Examples
The Bug (Before Fix)
body:
**User Input:**
```
TekExpress PCI
```

**Generated Code (WRONG):**
```python
msob.commands.application.activate.write(TekExpress PCI)
```

This is **invalid Python** - missing quotes around the string.

**What Should Be Generated:**
```python
msob.commands.application.activate.write("TekExpress PCI")
```

title: "No commands found"
body:
**Cause:** The tm_devices_full_tree.json file is missing or failed to load.

**Solution:** Ensure `/public/commands/tm_devices_full_tree.json` exists and is valid JSON.

title: Summary
body:
The tm_devices Command Browser transforms the complexity of the tm_devices API into an intuitive, hierarchical navigation experience. By enforcing valid command construction and providing model-specific validation, it eliminates common errors and accelerates workflow development.

**Access Points:**

✅ **Builder View:** Add tm_devices Command step → Browse Commands (adds to workflow)  
✅ **Commands Tab:** Click tm_devices Browser button (exploration/learning)

**Key Takeaways:**

✅ Use the browser for tm_devices API calls, not raw SCPI  
✅ Navigate the tree, don't search for SCPI strings  
✅ Provide indices explicitly when prompted  
✅ Preview generated code before adding  
✅ Combine with Python steps for advanced logic

For more information, see:

- [TM_DEVICES_API_REFERENCE.md](./TM_DEVICES_API_REFERENCE.md)
- [TEMPLATE_GUIDELINES.md](./TEMPLATE_GUIDELINES.md)
- [CUSTOM_GPT_SPECIFICATION.md](./CUSTOM_GPT_SPECIFICATION.md)

title: 1) `driver_mixins.shared_implementations`
body:
Path: `tm_devices.driver_mixins.shared_implementations`

Purpose:
- Shared mixins and IEEE488.2 command helper classes used by multiple device families.

Key classes:
- `CommonPISystemErrorCheckMixin`
- `CommonTSPErrorCheckMixin`
- `IEEE4882Commands`
- `LegacyTSPIEEE4882Commands`
- `TSPIEEE4882Commands`

Important behavior:
- `CommonPISystemErrorCheckMixin` provides PI-side error checking helpers (for example `expect_esr(...)`).
- Classes using this mixin must also include PI control capabilities (`write/query`) from the proper control mixin hierarchy.

How TekAutomate uses this:
- TekAutomate does **not** directly instantiate these mixins.
- They are consumed indirectly through the official `tm_devices` driver classes when generated Python calls high-level command APIs.

title: Internal AFG (Scope's Built-in Signal Generator)
body:
```python
from tm_devices import DeviceManager
from tm_devices.drivers import MSO5

with DeviceManager(verbose=True) as dm:
    scope: MSO5 = dm.add_scope("192.168.0.1")
    
    # Method 1: Individual commands
    scope.commands.afg.frequency.write(10e6)  # 10 MHz
    scope.commands.afg.offset.write(0.2)
    scope.commands.afg.square.duty.write(50)  # 50% duty cycle
    scope.commands.afg.function.write("SQUARE")
    scope.commands.afg.output.load.impedance.write("FIFTY")
    scope.commands.ch[1].scale.write(0.5, verify=True)
    scope.commands.afg.output.state.write(1)  # Turn on output
    scope.commands.esr.query()  # Check for errors
    
    # Method 2: Single method call
    scope.generate_function(
        frequency=10e6,
        offset=0.2,
        amplitude=0.5,
        duty_cycle=50,
        function=scope.source_device_constants.functions.SQUARE,
        termination="FIFTY",
    )
    scope.commands.ch[1].scale.write(0.5, verify=True)
    scope.commands.acquire.stopafter.write("SEQUENCE")
```

title: Error Checking Patterns
body:
```python
from tm_devices import DeviceManager
from tm_devices.drivers import MSO6B

with DeviceManager(verbose=True) as dm:
    scope: MSO6B = dm.add_scope("192.168.0.1")
    
    # Set and check command
    scope.set_and_check(":HORIZONTAL:SCALE", 100e-9)
    
    # Check error status register
    scope.expect_esr(0)
    
    # Query event status register
    esr_value = scope.commands.esr.query()
```

title: Notes for Custom GPT
body:
1. **Indexed Access**: Always use `[x]` brackets for indexed nodes (ch[1], meas[1], psu[1])
2. **String Indexed Access**: SMU channels use string indices: `smu["a"]`, `smu["b"]`
3. **Command Tree**: Commands follow hierarchical tree structure
4. **Write/Query**: Use `.write()` for commands, `.query()` for queries
5. **Verification**: Can add `verify=True` to write operations
6. **Helper Methods**: Some operations have high-level helper methods
7. **Context Manager**: Always use `with DeviceManager()` for proper cleanup
8. **Backend Selection**: Can specify VISA backend via `dm.visa_library`
9. **Aliases**: Devices can have aliases for easier reference
10. **Error Checking**: Use `set_and_check()`, `expect_esr()`, or `verify=True`
11. **OPC Pattern**: Use OPC query to wait for operation completion
12. **SMU Script Execution**: SMU supports direct script execution via `smu.write("script_code")`
13. **SMU Buffers**: Dynamic buffers created via script, accessed via `commands.buffer_var[NAME]`
14. **SMU Trigger Model**: Complex trigger sequences for waveform generation
15. **AWG Constraints**: Check waveform constraints before generation
16. **AFG Symmetry**: AFG supports symmetry parameter for certain waveforms

---

title: Blockly Block Implications
body:
When generating Blockly blocks for tm_devices operations:

1. **Channel Operations**: Use indexed channel blocks (ch[1], ch[2], etc.)
2. **Measurement Blocks**: Support indexed measurements (meas[1], meas[2], etc.)
3. **SMU Channel Blocks**: Support string-indexed SMU channels ("a", "b")
4. **SMU Source Blocks**: Support source function selection (DCVOLTS, DCAMPS)
5. **SMU Buffer Blocks**: Support dynamic buffer creation and access
6. **AWG/AFG Function Blocks**: Support function generation with constraints checking
7. **Verification**: Include optional verification checkbox
8. **OPC Waiting**: Provide wait_for_opc blocks after critical operations
9. **Helper Methods**: Prefer helper methods when available (save_screenshot, curve_query, generate_function)
10. **Context Awareness**: Blocks should track device context automatically
11. **Error Handling**: Support error checking blocks (expect_esr, set_and_check)
12. **SMU Trigger Blocks**: Support trigger model configuration for complex sequences
13. **AWG Constraints Blocks**: Support waveform constraint checking before generation

title: UnicodeDecodeError on Screenshot — *OPC? after FILESYSTEM:READFILE reads leftover PNG binary
body:
The PNG file data (binary bytes like 0xe5) was being interpreted as ASCII text. The sequence was: 1) scope.write('FILESYSTEM:READFILE "..."') 2) scope.read_raw() reads PNG data 3) scope.write('FILESYSTEM:DELETE "..."') 4) scope.query('*OPC?') tries to read '1' but gets leftover PNG data. The fix removes all query('*OPC?') calls after HARDCOPY START and FILESYSTEM:DELETE, replacing them with time.sleep(1.0). This avoids reading from the buffer entirely. Both legacy (HARDCOPY) and modern (SAVE:IMAGE) screenshot templates were updated.
UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xe5 in position 2. Occurs after screenshot capture when query('*OPC?') is called following FILESYSTEM:READFILE.
After FILESYSTEM:READFILE + read_raw(), leftover PNG binary data remains in the communication buffer. The subsequent scope.query('*OPC?') tries to read the expected '1' text response but instead reads leftover binary bytes, causing ASCII decode failure.
Replace all query('*OPC?') calls after file operations with time.sleep(1.0). Do not read from the buffer after binary transfers.
scope.write('HARDCOPY START')
scope.query('*OPC?')  # ❌ Tries to read response
time.sleep(0.5)
# ... file transfer ...
scope.write('FILESYSTEM:DELETE "..."')
scope.query('*OPC?')  # ❌ Reads leftover binary data = UnicodeDecodeError!
scope.write('HARDCOPY START')
time.sleep(1.0)  # ✅ Just wait, don't read
# ... file transfer ...
scope.write('FILESYSTEM:DELETE "..."')
# ✅ No query after delete, no error

title: Variable Name Corruption — Blockly getFieldValue('VAR') returns ID not name
Literal \n in python_code Blocks — Fix Applied Then Reversed
body:
This was a two-phase bug. First, literal 
 in python_code caused SyntaxError. A replacement was added: pythonCode = pythonCode.replace(/\n/g, '
'). But this broke string literals containing 
 escape sequences (e.g., f.write("data
") became f.write("data" followed by a raw newline, breaking the string). The final resolution was to remove the replacement entirely. The 
 sequences in Python string literals must remain as escape sequences in the output.
Phase 1: SyntaxError in generated Python because 
 is literal text not a newline. Phase 2 (after fix): String literals like f.write("data
") broken because the fix converted ALL 
 including those inside strings.
Phase 1: python_code block content like 'smu.source.function = "VOLT"
smu.output.enabled = True' had literal backslash-n characters instead of actual newlines, producing a SyntaxError. Phase 2: The fix (pythonCode.replace(/\n/g, '
')) broke legitimate escape sequences inside string literals like f.write("Frame,Frequency_Hz,Vpp_V
").
Final fix: Remove the 
 replacement entirely. The python_code block content should already contain actual newlines from the Blockly editor. The literal 
 issue was caused upstream, not in the generator.
// Phase 1 fix (CAUSED REGRESSION):
pythonCode = pythonCode.replace(/\n/g, '
');
// Final fix: removed the replacement entirely
// pythonCode is used as-is from the Blockly field

title: Float Loop Generation — range() Doesn't Accept Floats
body:
The generator now checks if any loop parameter contains a decimal point. If so, it generates: variable = start; while variable <= stop: ... variable += step. This handles voltage sweeps like 0.5V to 2.5V in 0.5V steps correctly. The Steps UI already generates while loops for sweeps, so this brings Blockly generation in line with Steps UI behavior.
Generated Python code contains 'for v in range(0.5, 2.5, 0.5)' which raises TypeError: 'float' object cannot be interpreted as an integer.
Python's built-in range() only accepts integer arguments. When Blockly controls_for blocks use float start/stop/step values (common in voltage sweeps), the generator produced invalid range() calls.
Detect float values in loop parameters. When any of start/stop/step are floats, generate a while loop instead of a for/range loop.
# BROKEN - range() rejects floats
for v in range(0.5, 2.5, 0.5):
    psu.write(f'VOLT {v}')
# FIXED - while loop for float ranges
v = 0.5
while v <= 2.5:
    psu.write(f'VOLT {v}')
    v += 0.5

title: Device Context Confusion — Blocks Inside Loops Can't Trace via getPreviousBlock()
Sweep Parameters Mismatch — Blockly Converter Uses Wrong Param Names for Steps UI
body:
For controls_repeat_ext/controls_repeat blocks: changed from {iterations, sweepType:'repeat'} to {variableName:'i', start:0, stop:iterations-1, step:1, saveResults:false}. For controls_for blocks: changed from {iterations, sweepType:'for', variable:varName} to {variableName:varName, start:fromValue, stop:toValue, step:byValue, saveResults:false}. The Steps UI generates sweep loops from App.tsx lines 4466-4501 using these exact param names.
Exported Steps from Blockly have undefined variables and incorrect loop structure. Generated Python shows 'while value <= 4:' instead of proper for loop, and variables like psu, voltage, current_device, i are undefined.
The Blockly-to-Steps converter (blockToStep.ts) was using iterations/sweepType params, but the Steps UI expects variableName/start/stop/step/saveResults. The param key names didn't match what App.tsx generatePython() reads.
Map Blockly loop block parameters to the correct Steps UI param names: variableName (not variable), start, stop, step, saveResults.
// OLD (WRONG)
params: {
  iterations,
  sweepType: 'for',
  variable: varName,
  start: fromValue,
  stop: toValue,
  step: byValue
}
// NEW (CORRECT)
params: {
  variableName: varName,
  start: fromValue,
  stop: toValue,
  step: byValue,
  saveResults: false
}

title: set_and_query Roundtrip Degradation — 4975 Commands at Risk
HARDCOPY:FORMAT Template Bug — Literal ${format} in Output Instead of PNG
OPC Query Return Type — int() Fails on String with Newline
body:
Changed all OPC query patterns from int() conversion to string comparison with strip(). This handles both '1' and '1\n' responses correctly across all backends.
int(scope.commands.opc.query()) fails with ValueError if OPC response is '1\n' (string with trailing newline).
OPC query returns a string, not an integer. The response may include trailing whitespace or newline. Calling int() directly on '1\n' works in some Python versions but fails in others or with certain VISA backends.
Use .strip() and string comparison instead of int() conversion.
if int(scope.commands.opc.query()) == 1:
    pass  # Acquisition complete
if scope.commands.opc.query().strip() == "1":
    pass  # Acquisition complete

title: Variables Initialized to None — Blockly Default Generator Creates Unnecessary Assignments
body:
The fix overrides Blockly's built-in variable initialization behavior. Instead of emitting 'variable = None' for every declared variable, the generator now skips initialization entirely. Variables get their values when they are first assigned through variables_set blocks or as loop indices. This produces cleaner code and makes it easier to spot actual variable usage patterns.
Generated Python starts with 'vpp = None', 'frame = None', 'hits = None' — unnecessary initialization that clutters code and can mask real assignment bugs.
Blockly's default Python generator automatically initializes all variables declared in the <variables> section of the XML to None. This happens before any user code is generated.
Override the variables_get generator to prevent None initialization. Variables are now only assigned when explicitly set via variables_set blocks.
# Blockly default output:
vpp = None
frame = None
hits = None

# ... actual code starts here
# No pre-initialization
# Variables assigned where first used:
vpp = float(scope.query(':MEASUREMENT:IMMED:VALUE?').strip())

title: Missing Cleanup Section — Devices Not Properly Closed
body:
The cleanup section now generates: 1) close() call for each connected device with an if-in-locals() guard, 2) DeviceManager.close() for tm_devices workflows, 3) ResourceManager.close() for PyVISA workflows. The guard pattern (if 'scope' in locals()) ensures cleanup doesn't fail if the script errored before device connection.
Generated Python scripts don't close device connections. Resources leak, VISA sessions may become exhausted, instruments may remain locked.
The generator did not always emit a cleanup section at the end of the script. When devices were connected but no explicit disconnect block was present, the script would end without closing connections.
Always generate a cleanup section when any device connections are present in the workflow.
# Script ends without cleanup
scope.write('SAVE:WAVEFORM ...')
# EOF - resources leak!
# Cleanup - close all devices
if 'scope' in locals():
    scope.close()
    print('Disconnected scope')

if 'device_manager' in locals():
    device_manager.close()
    print('DeviceManager closed')

title: Backend Selection Not Preserved on XML Import — Dropdown Shows PyVISA While Internal State Is tm_devices
GPT Device Context Errors — Scope Commands Sent to SMU Context Repeatedly
body:
This is a persistent GPT behavior that required 4+ iterations to fix per user interaction. The instructions now include: RULE: Command prefix determines context — CH1:|ACQuire:|MEASU:|DATa: → (scope), :SOURce:|:OUTPut:|:MEASure: → (smu)/(psu). A post-processing validator in TekAutomate was recommended that flags incorrect device contexts before import. Long-term: consider fine-tuning or a specialized two-pass prompt (first generate DEVICE_CONTEXT_MAP, then validate each block).
GPT-generated XML has scope commands (CH1:SCALE, ACQuire:STATE ON) with DEVICE_CONTEXT=(smu). Workflow completely fails — SMU receives invalid commands, scope is never configured.
The Custom GPT does not reliably track which SCPI commands belong to which instrument type. It defaults to the most recently mentioned device for ALL commands, mixing contexts across instrument boundaries.
Enhanced GPT system instructions (v6, 7995 chars) with: 1) Explicit wrong/correct visual examples, 2) Command prefix→device mapping rules (CH1:|ACQuire: → scope, :SOURce:|:OUTPut: → smu/psu), 3) Mandatory validation checklist before generating XML.
<!-- ❌ WRONG - Scope command sent to SMU -->
<block type="scpi_write">
  <field name="DEVICE_CONTEXT">(smu)</field>
  <field name="COMMAND">CH1:SCAle 1.0</field>
</block>
<!-- ✅ CORRECT - Context matches command -->
<block type="scpi_write">
  <field name="DEVICE_CONTEXT">(scope)</field>
  <field name="COMMAND">CH1:SCAle 1.0</field>
</block>

title: IP Conflict Detection — Generator Throws Error But No UI-Level Warning
body:
The generator correctly detects when multiple connect_scope blocks use the same IP/resource string and throws a descriptive error. However, users discover this only when they click 'Generate Python', not while building the workflow. Recommendation: add real-time validation in BlocklyBuilder UI to highlight devices with duplicate IPs as they are configured.
When two devices share the same IP address, the generator throws 'RESOURCE COLLISION DETECTED' with fix instructions, but the user sees no visual warning in the Blockly or Steps UI before attempting generation.
IP collision detection exists only at the generator level (pythonGenerators.ts lines 257-276). There is no pre-generation validation in the Blockly or Steps UI to catch this earlier.
Status: generator-level detection is implemented. UI-level validation (highlighting/flagging duplicate IPs before generation) is recommended but not yet implemented.

title: Socket Daemon State Machine — Why Binary Transfer Over Port 4000 Works
body:
Four requirements for successful binary transfer: 1) EXACT COMMAND ORDERING — queries like SAVE:IMAGe:FILEFormat?, SAVE:IMAGe:COMPosition?, etc. prime internal UI subsystems; without them the SAVE:IMAGe pipeline is half-initialized. 2) HARD RESET between every step — *CLS before each query forces status clear, event queue clear, UI handler reset. 3) ONE-SHOT BINARY WINDOW — after sending FILESystem:READFile, do NOT query anything, do NOT call *OPC?, do NOT send *CLS; let the socket stay in raw streaming mode until timeout. 4) HEADER RESYNCHRONIZATION — the socket may prepend stray ASCII/newlines; realign on PNG magic bytes (\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n) and discard garbage. PyVISA fails because: it treats timeout as error not EOF, expects block headers or termination that READFILE doesn't provide, and cannot safely guess stream-based completion semantics.
FILESYSTEM:READFILE fails over PyVISA (timeout or UnicodeDecodeError) but succeeds over raw TCP sockets with exact sequencing. Common belief: 'READFILE does not work on raw sockets' is operationally true for naive scripts but false for primed pipelines.
FILESYSTEM:READFILE is intentionally non-IEEE-488.2-compliant. It is a command (not a query) that produces unframed binary output with no length header, no termination character, and termination only by EOI. VISA cannot determine when the read is complete, so it times out and raises VI_ERROR_TMO. Raw sockets can treat timeout as EOF.
Use raw TCP socket with exact pipeline priming sequence. Document as: 'Raw-socket screenshot capture requires exact pipeline priming. Do not reorder or remove queries.'
# PyVISA approach — FAILS
scope.write('FILESYSTEM:READFILE "C:/Temp/screenshot.png"')
data = scope.read_raw()  # ❌ Times out, raises VI_ERROR_TMO
# Raw socket approach — WORKS
sock.sendall(b'FILESystem:READFile "C:/Temp/screenshot.png"\n')
sock.settimeout(5)
data = bytearray()
while True:
    try:
        chunk = sock.recv(65536)
        if chunk: data.extend(chunk)
        else: break
    except socket.timeout:
        if len(data) > 0: break  # Timeout = EOF
# Realign on PNG header
png_magic = b'\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n'
if data[:8] != png_magic and png_magic in data:
    data = data[data.find(png_magic):]

title: Screenshot Template Wrong Structure — Multi-Step Sequences Broken in Steps UI
Setup.bat Stops After Node.js Detection — errorlevel vs %ERRORLEVEL%
body:
The fixed script: 1) Uses 'errorlevel' checks instead of '%ERRORLEVEL%' variable for reliable error detection, 2) Redirects stderr (2>nul) to prevent error messages from stopping the script, 3) Explicitly jumps to the next section with goto statements, 4) Captures npm exit codes in a separate variable. Manual workaround: run 'npm install' or 'npm install --legacy-peer-deps' directly.
SETUP.bat prints 'Node.js found!' with version info, then stops silently. Never proceeds to step 3 (npm install).
The batch script used %ERRORLEVEL% variable checks which behave differently from 'if errorlevel' syntax. Version commands (node --version, npm --version) may set error levels that halt script flow. Additionally, stderr output from these commands could interfere with flow control.
Updated script to use 'if errorlevel' checks instead of '%ERRORLEVEL%' variable, redirect stderr, and add explicit 'goto :install_deps' jumps between sections.
node --version
if %ERRORLEVEL% NEQ 0 (
  echo Node.js not found
)
node --version 2>nul
if errorlevel 1 (
  echo Node.js not found
  goto :end
)
goto :install_deps

title: ZIP Missing Commands/Templates — Nested File Inclusion Bug
body:
Verification: run scripts\VERIFY_ZIP.bat after creating distribution. Expected output: 'public/commands/: 17 files', 'public/templates/: 6 files'. If extraction fails, use Windows built-in extraction (right-click → Extract All) or 7-Zip with 'Preserve folder structure' enabled.
After extracting distribution ZIP, public/commands/ or public/templates/ folders are empty. App has no SCPI commands available.
The distribution script (CREATE_DISTRIBUTION.bat) did not properly include nested files from public/commands/ and public/templates/ directories in the ZIP archive.
Updated CREATE_DISTRIBUTION.bat to properly include nested files. Added VERIFY_ZIP.bat script to confirm: 17 command files and 6 template files present. Added helper file count display.

title: Electron Loading Error — Missing electron-is-dev, Protocol Intercept, webSecurity
body:
Electron requires special handling for local resources. The fetch() API expects http/https protocols; file:// protocol requires a custom protocol intercept registered via protocol.registerFileProtocol() or protocol.handle(). webSecurity: false is needed for development but should be true in production with proper CSP headers.
Electron app fails to load with blank screen or fetch() errors. Resources fail to load from local filesystem.
Three issues: 1) Missing electron-is-dev dependency, 2) fetch() API doesn't work with file:// protocol in Electron without custom protocol intercept, 3) webSecurity settings block local resource loading.
Install electron-is-dev, register custom protocol handler for local file serving, configure webSecurity appropriately for development vs production.

title: Windows Defender Blocking — Unsigned Portable Exe
body:
The recommended distribution method is the win-unpacked folder rather than a single portable .exe. Users extract the folder and run the executable inside. This avoids both SmartScreen warnings and antivirus false positives. Long-term fix: obtain a code signing certificate for Tektronix-distributed builds.
Windows Defender or SmartScreen blocks TekAutomate.exe from running. User sees 'Windows protected your PC' dialog.
The portable .exe is unsigned (no code signing certificate). Windows Defender flags unsigned executables, especially ones downloaded from the internet.
Distribute the win-unpacked folder instead of a single .exe. Unpacked folder contents are less likely to trigger antivirus heuristics.

title: Undefined Variables in Generated Code — Steps UI Multi-Device Export
body:
The Blockly Python output was correct (proper for loop, variable calculations, device-specific references). The Steps UI output was broken (undefined psu, voltage, current_device, i). Root cause was the converter not properly translating Blockly's per-block device binding to Steps UI's global model. Fix ensures: 1) Connect blocks create proper device entries, 2) Variable calculations become python steps, 3) Device-specific commands map to the correct device name.
Generated Python from Steps UI (after Blockly export) has undefined variables: psu, voltage, current_device, i. Code references devices and variables that were never declared.
When Blockly exports to Steps UI, device connections may not map correctly. Blockly tracks device context per block, but Steps UI uses a global device connection model. Variable calculations from Blockly (e.g., voltage = 1 + i * 0.5) become python steps that may lose their variable scope.
Fixed blockToStep.ts converter to properly map device connections, variable declarations, and calculations. Connect steps now carry device configuration. Variable calculations exported as python step type.
# Steps UI generated (BROKEN):
while value <= 4:
    psu.write('VOLT 1.0')  # psu undefined!
    current_device.write('ACQUIRE:STATE ON')  # generic!
# After fix:
for i in range(5):
    voltage = 1 + i * 0.5
    psu.write(f'VOLT {voltage}')  # correct device + variable
    scope.write('ACQUIRE:STATE ON')  # device-specific

title: Wrong Loop Structure — while value <= 4 Instead of Proper for Loop
body:
Steps UI sweep generation (App.tsx lines 4466-4501) always produces while loops: 'variableName = start; while variableName <= stop: ... variableName += step'. This is correct behavior, but requires the variableName parameter to be set. Without it, the generator uses a generic 'value' variable. The fix in blockToStep.ts ensures variableName is always populated from the Blockly loop's VAR field.
Steps UI generates 'while value <= 4:' with unnamed variable 'value' instead of 'for i in range(5):' from the original Blockly loop.
The sweep conversion from Blockly controls_for to Steps UI sweep step lost the variable name and loop structure. Steps UI always generates while loops for sweeps, but the variable name ('i') wasn't carried through.
Ensure variableName parameter is correctly mapped in the blockToStep converter so Steps UI while loops use the correct variable name from the Blockly source.
# WRONG - generic variable name
value = 0
while value <= 4:
    psu.write('VOLT 1.0')  # no variable reference!
    value += 1
# CORRECT - proper variable name carried through
i = 0
while i <= 4:
    voltage = (1 + (i * 0.5))
    psu.write(f'VOLT {voltage}')
    i += 1

title: Missing Device Connections — Blockly→Steps Export Drops Connect Blocks
Redundant Imports — PyVISA Imported Multiple Times
body:
The generator now collects all needed imports during the block traversal phase, then emits them once in the header. This applies to pyvisa, time, tm_devices (DeviceManager), os, pathlib, and any other imports. The dedup also handles the case where both 'import pyvisa' and 'from pyvisa import ResourceManager' might be generated.
Generated Python contains 'import pyvisa' multiple times, once per device connection block.
Each connect block independently generated its import statement without checking if it had already been emitted.
Deduplicate imports at generation time. Collect all required imports first, then emit unique imports once at the top of the script.
import pyvisa
rm = pyvisa.ResourceManager()
scope = rm.open_resource(...)
import pyvisa  # ❌ Duplicate!
rm = pyvisa.ResourceManager()
psu = rm.open_resource(...)
import pyvisa

rm = pyvisa.ResourceManager()
scope = rm.open_resource(...)
psu = rm.open_resource(...)

title: Acquire Stopafter Enum — SEQUENCE vs SEQuence Mixed Case
Verbose OPC Wait Pattern — Polling Loop Instead of Simple Write/Read for tm_devices
body:
The polling pattern (start_time = time.time(); while time.time() - start_time < 30: if scope.query('*OPC?').strip() == '1': break; time.sleep(0.1)) was replaced with a simpler pattern for tm_devices. Detection is based on the device backend setting.
Generated tm_devices code uses a 10-line polling loop for OPC wait instead of a clean 2-line pattern.
Generator used a universal polling pattern (while loop with time check) for all backends, even tm_devices which supports a cleaner write/read pattern.
For tm_devices: generate scope.write('*OPC?') + scope.read() pattern. For PyVISA: keep polling pattern (required for some VISA implementations).
# Verbose polling (tm_devices)
start_time = time.time()
while time.time() - start_time < 30:
    if scope.query('*OPC?').strip() == '1':
        break
    time.sleep(0.1)
else:
    print('Warning: OPC timeout on scope')
# Clean pattern (tm_devices)
scope.write('*OPC?')
response = scope.read()
if response.strip() != '1':
    print('Warning: OPC did not return 1 on scope')

title: Missing/Wrong Type Hints for tm_devices — Default MSO6B Applied Universally
body:
Without DRIVER_NAME in XML: scope = device_manager.add_scope('192.168.1.10'). With DRIVER_NAME='MSO6B' in XML: scope: MSO6B = device_manager.add_scope('192.168.1.10'). This prevents incorrect type hints from causing confusion or import errors.
Generated code always has 'scope: MSO6B = device_manager.add_scope(...)' even when no driver name was specified in the XML.
Generator defaulted DRIVER_NAME to 'MSO6B' when not explicitly set in the Blockly XML, producing incorrect type hints.
Changed default from 'MSO6B' to empty string. Type hints only added when DRIVER_NAME is explicitly provided in XML.
// Default always applied
scope: MSO6B = device_manager.add_scope("192.168.1.10")
// No type hint without explicit DRIVER_NAME
scope = device_manager.add_scope("192.168.1.10")

title: OPC Dedup — Single Acquisition + OPC Wait Not Combined
body:
The combined pattern uses ACQUIRE:STOPAFTER SEQUENCE + ACQUIRE:STATE ON;*OPC? + read() which blocks until acquisition is complete. This is more efficient and idiomatic than separate stop-after, state-run, and polling-loop sequences.
Generated code has separate single_acquisition and wait_for_opc blocks producing redundant command sequences instead of a combined efficient pattern.
single_acquisition and wait_for_opc were independently generating their command sequences without awareness of adjacent blocks.
single_acquisition now detects if the next block is wait_for_opc. If detected, it generates a combined command. wait_for_opc detects single_acquisition as previous block and defers to it.
scope.write('ACQUIRE:STOPAFTER SEQUENCE')
scope.write('ACQUIRE:STATE RUN')
# Wait for operation complete
start_time = time.time()
while time.time() - start_time < 30:
    if scope.query('*OPC?').strip() == '1': break
    time.sleep(0.1)
scope.write('ACQUIRE:STOPAFTER SEQUENCE')
scope.write('ACQUIRE:STATE ON;*OPC?')
scope.read()  # Block until acquisition complete

title: GPT Missing Connection Details — connect_scope Blocks Without IP Addresses
body:
Added to GPT validation checklist: 1) All connect_scope blocks have IP addresses, 2) All SCPI commands use correct DEVICE_CONTEXT, 3) Count instruments vs disconnects. GPT must now self-validate before providing XML.
GPT-generated XML has connect_scope blocks without RESOURCE field containing the IP address. Blocks won't actually connect to instruments.
GPT instructions didn't explicitly require IP addresses in every connect_scope block. GPT omitted the RESOURCE field or the mutation element.
Updated GPT instructions to mandate: connect_scope: DEVICE_NAME, IP, BACKEND — IP ALWAYS REQUIRED. Include mutation: <mutation show_advanced="true" current_backend="pyvisa"></mutation>.
<block type="connect_scope">
  <field name="DEVICE_NAME">scope</field>
  <field name="BACKEND">pyvisa</field>
  <!-- MISSING: RESOURCE field! -->
</block>
<block type="connect_scope">
  <field name="DEVICE_NAME">scope</field>
  <field name="BACKEND">pyvisa</field>
  <field name="RESOURCE">TCPIP::192.168.1.100::INSTR</field>
  <mutation show_advanced="true" current_backend="pyvisa"></mutation>
</block>

title: GPT Variable Substitution Assumptions — ${voltage} in SCPI Commands
body:
For SCPI commands with dynamic values, the recommended approach is to use a python_code block with proper f-string: scope.write(f'VOLT {voltage}'). The ${} syntax in SCPI command fields is converted by the generator but may have edge cases.
GPT generates XML with ${voltage} syntax in SCPI command fields. May not be supported in all TekAutomate code paths.
GPT assumed JavaScript template literal syntax (${variable}) works universally in SCPI command strings. This works in filename fields but may need different handling (Python f-string) for SCPI commands.
GPT instructions updated to use Python f-string syntax in python_code blocks instead of ${} in SCPI command fields.
<field name="COMMAND">:SOURce:VOLTage:LEVel ${voltage}</field>
<!-- Use python_code block instead -->
scope.write(f':SOURce:VOLTage:LEVel {voltage}')

title: Backend Compatibility Validation — scpi_write/scpi_query FORBIDDEN with tm_devices
body:
The generator walks all blocks and checks compatibility: scpi_write and scpi_query blocks are only valid with pyvisa backend. With tm_devices, users must use the command-tree blocks or python_code blocks. The validation report shows each violating command so users can fix them before generation.
Generator produces errors listing SCPI commands that are incompatible with the selected backend. E.g., raw scpi_write blocks cannot be used with tm_devices backend.
tm_devices uses a command tree API (scope.commands.horizontal.scale.write()) not raw SCPI strings (scope.write('HOR:SCALE 1e-6')). Using scpi_write blocks with tm_devices backend produces code that won't work.
Generator detects backend and validates that block types are compatible. Lists all violations with specific command names and suggests switching to appropriate block types or backend.

title: Loop Index Variable Not Available — repeat N Times Block Has No Index
body:
The repeat N times block is designed for simple repetition without needing an index. For cases requiring an index (dynamic filenames like capture_{i}.wfm, voltage calculations like 1.0 + i * 0.5), users must use the 'count with i from 1 to 5 by 1' block instead. This is a Blockly design choice, not a TekAutomate bug.
Using 'repeat 5 times' block produces 'for _ in range(5):' with no loop index variable. Cannot use index for dynamic filenames or voltage calculations.
Blockly's controls_repeat_ext block uses '_' as the loop variable (convention for unused). Users expect to reference the iteration count for dynamic values like filenames or voltage steps.
Users should use controls_for block (count with i) instead of controls_repeat_ext. The for block provides a named index variable.
# repeat 5 times block - no index
for _ in range(5):
    psu.write('VOLT 1.0')  # Always 1.0!
    scope.write('SAVE:WAVEFORM CH1, "capture.wfm"')  # Overwrites!
# count with i block - has index
for i in range(5):
    voltage = 1.0 + (i * 0.5)
    psu.write(f'VOLT {voltage}')  # 1.0, 1.5, 2.0...
    scope.write(f'SAVE:WAVEFORM CH1, "capture_{i}.wfm"')  # Unique files

title: File Overwriting in Loop — Same Filename Every Iteration
body:
When using save_waveform or save_screenshot blocks inside a loop, the filename is static. Users must replace the save block with a python_code block that constructs a dynamic filename using the loop variable. This is documented in the examples (example_scope_psu_sweep.xml) which use f-strings for unique filenames per iteration.
Waveform or screenshot save inside a loop always writes to the same filename, overwriting previous captures. Only the last iteration's data survives.
save_waveform and save_screenshot blocks use a static filename field. Inside a loop, the same filename is used every iteration.
Use python_code block with f-string to include loop index in filename: scope.write(f'SAVE:WAVEFORM CH1, "C:/Captures/capture_{i}.wfm"')
# Static filename - overwrites every iteration
for i in range(5):
    scope.write('SAVE:WAVEFORM CH1, "C:/Captures/capture.wfm"')
# Dynamic filename - unique per iteration
for i in range(5):
    scope.write(f'SAVE:WAVEFORM CH1, "C:/Captures/capture_{i}.wfm"')

title: Comment vs Python Code Block — User Confusion
body:
Comment blocks generate '# Set PSU voltage (START_VOLTAGE + i * VOLTAGE_STEP)' which is a Python comment and does nothing. For actual calculation, users need a python_code block containing 'voltage = START_VOLTAGE + i * VOLTAGE_STEP' followed by an scpi_write using f-string: psu.write(f'VOLT {voltage}').
User adds a comment block containing 'Set PSU voltage (START_VOLTAGE + i * VOLTAGE_STEP)' expecting it to calculate the voltage. Nothing happens because it's just a comment.
Users confuse Blockly comment blocks (which produce Python # comments) with python_code blocks (which produce executable Python). A comment that looks like code does nothing.
Replace comment blocks with python_code blocks for actual calculations. Add user guidance distinguishing comments from executable code.
# Comment block output (does nothing):
# Set PSU voltage (START_VOLTAGE + i * VOLTAGE_STEP)
psu.write('VOLT 1.0')  # Static value, comment was ignored
# python_code block output (executes):
voltage = 1.0 + (i * 0.5)  # Real calculation
psu.write(f'VOLT {voltage}')  # Dynamic value

title: Export File Naming — Ugly Names with Spaces and Dashes
SCPI Corpus Tests — Every Command → Step → Python Validation (~13k Commands)
body:
npm run test:product loads every command from every JSON file (8 device families: afg, awg, smu, rsa, tekexpress, dpojet, MSO_DPO_5k_7k_70K, mso_2_4_5_6_7), builds a step, runs through the real generator, validates Python output. Per command checks: generator doesn't throw, scpi.write( or scpi.query( present, no unresolved {param} placeholders, SCPI command header appears in output. Results grouped by device family + command group with confidence score. Separately, npm run test:param-pipeline validates ~8,793 parameterized commands ensuring every {param} resolves correctly.
N/A — test infrastructure pattern

title: Parameter Pipeline Tests — Every {param} Resolves Correctly (~8k Commands)
body:
npm run test:param-pipeline tests every command with {param} placeholders: substitutes realistic test values, asserts the resolved value appears in generated Python, asserts no {param} remains unresolved, reports which commands are partially or fully broken. Uses AJV schema validation: every command must have unique identifier, commands with {param} in SCPI string must declare matching arguments/params entries, no orphaned placeholders allowed.

title: E2E Coverage Matrix — What's Covered and Known Gaps
body:
Covered: Steps-only (palette actions, save waveform options, save screenshot, recall), Blockly-only (toolbar actions, browse commands modal, code generation), Roundtrip/Fidelity (Steps→Blockly→Steps for write/query, set_and_query preservation canary). Known gaps: 1) Exhaustive option permutations for each step type, 2) Full Blockly block option permutations, 3) Save Waveform advanced options across Steps↔Blockly, 4) Multi-device binding permutations, 5) Negative-path UX assertions (disabled states, validation messages, malformed inputs). Test files: default-controls.spec.ts, flow-fidelity.spec.ts, set-and-query-canary.spec.ts, regression.spec.ts, scpi-corpus.spec.ts.

title: Snapshot Regression Tests — Generated Python Frozen for Known Step Sequences
body:
generatorSnapshots.test.ts freezes generated Python output for known step sequences. Any change to generator output causes snapshot mismatch, immediately flagging regressions. Combined with 14 named regression scenarios in regression.spec.ts covering: SCPI command string integrity (R1.1-R1.6), set_and_query roundtrip (R6.1-R6.4), and import/template load regression (R7.1-R7.4). Run with: npm run test:regression (~60s, most targeted test for generator changes).
N/A — regression detection pattern
N/A — test infrastructure
N/A — test implementation

title: Regression Tests — SCPI Command String Integrity Scenarios
body:
R1.1: MSO CH1:SCAle with value 1.0 — catches {value} unresolved. R1.2: MSO SAVEONEVent:WAVEform:SOUrce with CH3 — catches {CH<x>} or <x> leaking. R1.3: AFG SOURce1:FREQuency:FIXed with 1000 — catches {ch}/{freq} unsubstituted. R1.4: AWG OUTPut1:STATe with ON — catches {0|1|OFF|ON} options string in output. R1.5: SMU :MEASure:VOLTage:DC? — catches query commands silently dropped. R1.6: RSA INITIATE:CONTINUOUS with OFF — catches {state} not resolved.
Regression scenarios R1.1-R1.6 catch: {value} placeholder left unresolved, <x> token leaking into output, {ch}/{freq} not substituted, options string {0|1|OFF|ON} appearing in output, query commands silently dropped, enumeration {state} not resolved.
Generator parameter substitution can regress when code changes affect the substituteSCPI or genSteps functions.
Automated regression tests run on every push. Each scenario exports Python and checks for specific resolved values.

title: Regression Tests — set_and_query Roundtrip Scenarios
body:
R6.1: CH1:SCAle 2.0 — both write('CH1:SCAle 2.0') and query('CH1:SCAle?') present. R6.2: ACQuire:MODe — write always appears before query. R6.3: CH1:COUPling (empty value) — header-only command doesn't crash, both emitted. R6.4: CH1:SCAle + CH2:SCAle — two steps produce two independent pairs, no bleed-over. Run with: npm run test:regression.
Regression scenarios R6.1-R6.4 catch: missing query after write, wrong ordering (query before write), header-only command crash, multi-step bleed-over between pairs.
set_and_query produces both write('CMD value') and query('CMD?'). Either half can be lost during conversion or generation.
Automated regression tests validate both write and query appear, in correct order, for each set_and_query step.

title: Regression Tests — Import/Template Load Roundtrip Scenarios
body:
R7.1: Export → save flow JSON → import (replace) → re-export — Python must be byte-identical. R7.2: Import flow JSON → export Python — all commands survive. R7.3: Import in append mode — existing steps + imported steps both present. R7.4: Load Hello Scope template → export — *IDN?, *OPT? present, Python valid. Run with: npm run test:regression.
Regression scenarios R7.1-R7.4 catch: import mutating step data, commands lost on roundtrip, append mode failure, template loading issues.
Import/export cycle can corrupt step data through mutation, field loss, or incorrect merge logic.
Four regression scenarios validate import/export fidelity: export→import→re-export byte-identical, all commands survive roundtrip, append mode preserves existing + imported, template loads produce expected commands.

title: XML wait_seconds Using <value> Instead of <field> — Python Generation Fails
body:
When a Blockly block definition uses FieldNumber, the XML must use <field name="SECONDS">2</field>, not <value name="SECONDS"><shadow ...>. The <value> format is for input connections (pluggable blocks), while <field> is for inline field values. The visual rendering may look similar but the generator accesses them differently: block.getFieldValue('SECONDS') vs block.getInputValue('SECONDS').
TekExpress_USB.xml wait_seconds blocks fail to generate Python. Block appears correct in UI but produces no output.
wait_seconds block definition uses FieldNumber (a <field> element), but the XML used <value> with shadow blocks. Blockly renders it visually but the generator doesn't find the expected field.
Changed all 6 instances in TekExpress_USB.xml from <value name="SECONDS"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">2</field></shadow></value> to <field name="SECONDS">2</field>.
<block type="wait_seconds" id="tx_wait_1">
  <value name="SECONDS">
    <shadow type="math_number" id="num_2s_1">
      <field name="NUM">2</field>
    </shadow>
  </value>
</block>
<block type="wait_seconds" id="tx_wait_1">
  <field name="SECONDS">2</field>
</block>

title: XML Missing TERMINATION Field — configure_channel Block Incomplete
body:
The configure_channel block now generates scope.write('CH1:TERMINATION ONEMEG') in addition to SCALE, OFFSET, and COUPLING commands. Without the field, the termination command was silently omitted.
basic_setup_waveform.xml configure_channel block missing TERMINATION field. Block definition updated to include termination but XML not updated.
Block definition for configure_channel was updated to include a TERMINATION field (50Ω vs 1MΩ selection), but the example XML file was not updated to match.
Added <field name="TERMINATION">ONEMEG</field> to the configure_channel block in basic_setup_waveform.xml.
<block type="configure_channel">
  <field name="CHANNEL">CH1</field>
  <field name="SCALE">1.0</field>
  <field name="OFFSET">0.0</field>
  <field name="COUPLING">DC</field>
</block>
<block type="configure_channel">
  <field name="CHANNEL">CH1</field>
  <field name="SCALE">1.0</field>
  <field name="OFFSET">0.0</field>
  <field name="COUPLING">DC</field>
  <field name="TERMINATION">ONEMEG</field>
</block>

title: Transport Selection Rule — VISA vs Raw Socket Per Command Type
body:
Decision matrix: *IDN?, ACQ:MODE, MEASU:IMM? → PyVISA (message-based). HARDCopy:DATA? → PyVISA INSTR only (definite-length block). SAVE:IMAGe + READFile → raw socket (file-based stream). FILESystem:READFile → raw socket (unframed binary). CURVE? (large) → raw socket preferred. Hard fail: agent must refuse to execute FILESystem:READFile over PyVISA. Mixed-transport workflows are expected and correct — use PyVISA for setup/measurement, switch to raw socket for binary transfers.
Binary file transfers fail over PyVISA. Timeout errors on READFILE. UnicodeDecodeError on mixed binary/text traffic.
VISA enforces message-based completion rules (termination character, block header, or driver-level end condition). FILESYSTEM:READFILE produces unframed binary with no such markers. These are fundamentally incompatible.
Mandatory transport selection: PyVISA for message-based operations (config, measurements, queries); raw TCP socket for stream-based operations (screenshots, file transfer, binary data). AI agents must inspect each SCPI command and select transport accordingly.

title: Python Runtime Validation — Generated Code Compiles and Runs with Mock PyVISA
body:
npm run test:python-validate generates a script for a known step sequence, runs python -m py_compile (syntax check), then runs it with a mock PyVISA module (no hardware needed). Skips gracefully if Python is not installed. This catches: syntax errors in generated Python, import errors, undefined variable references, and basic runtime errors. Does not catch SCPI-level errors since instruments are mocked.
N/A — validation infrastructure
N/A — test infrastructure
N/A — test implementation

title: GPT Missing Loop Variable Mutation in XML — controls_for Without <mutation>
body:
Without the mutation element, Blockly may: 1) Not properly register the loop variable in its variable model, 2) Break variable scoping, 3) Cause unstable XML roundtrip import/export. The generator uses the VAR field as a fallback so code generation still works, but the Blockly UI behavior may be incorrect.
GPT-generated XML has controls_for blocks with <field name="VAR">frame</field> but missing <mutation><variable>frame</variable></mutation>. Blockly may not properly track the loop variable.
Custom GPT instructions did not include the mutation element in loop block examples. GPT generates the VAR field but omits the mutation.
Updated GPT instructions to require mutation in controls_for blocks. Generator also handles this gracefully by using VAR field as fallback when mutation is missing.
<block type="controls_for">
  <field name="VAR">frame</field>
  <!-- MISSING mutation -->
</block>
<block type="controls_for">
  <field name="VAR">frame</field>
  <mutation><variable>frame</variable></mutation>
</block>
